coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of chromium(VI) in real samples based on dual emissive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yunxia
Chen, Yonglei
Liu, Juanjuan
Han, Yangxia
Ma, Sudai
Chen, Xingguo
coredata.dc:description: As we know, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) was usually used as an additive to improve the color fastness during the printing and dyeing process, and thus posing tremendous threat to our health and living quality. In this work, the dual emissive carbon dots (DECDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of m-aminophenol and oxalic acid. The obtained DECDs not only exhibited dual emission fluorescence peaks (430 nm, 510 nm) under the single excitation wavelength of 380 nm, but also possessed good water solubility and excellent fluorescence stability. A ratiometric fluorescent method for the determination of Cr(VI) was developed using the DECDs as a probe. Under the optimal conditions, a linear range was obtained from 2 to 300 μM with a limit of detection of 0.4 μM. Furthermore, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent method was applied to the analysis of Cr(VI) in textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium residue samples with satisfactory recoveries (88.4–106.8%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emissive carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Cr(VI) detection
Real samples

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” assay for the detection of Cr(VI)
Analysis of Cr(VI) in real samples
Preparation of dual emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
Fluorescent response property of DECDs toward Cr(VI)
Detection of Cr(VI) in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the DECDs, (B) the size distribution histogram of the DECDs.
Characterization of structure and functional groups of the DECDs. The XPS high resolution (A) C 1s, (B) N 1s and (C) O 1s spectra of the DECDs, respectively. (D) FT-IR spectrum of the DECDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the DECDs (Inset is the large version of A), (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the DECDs with different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Stability of the DECDs in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl. (B) Photostability of the DECDs under the 380 nm xenon lamp equipped in the fluorescence spectrometer. (C) The F 510/F 430 of the DECDs at different pH values. (D) The F 510/F 430 of the DECDs incubated at different temperatures for 5 min.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the DECDs in the presence of various concentrations of Cr(VI) (from top to bottom: 0, 2, 15, 25, 40, 60, 100, 200, 300 μM). (B) Linear plots of F 510/F 430 of the DECDs as a function of Cr(VI) concentration, the error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

(A) F 510/F 430 response of the DECDs to 500 μM metal ions (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM Cr(VI) (red bars). (B) F 510/F 430 response of the DECDs to 500 μM anion ions (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM Cr(VI) (red bars). (C) F 510/F 430 response of the DECDs to 500 μM catechol, resorcinol, glycine, alanine, cysteine, glutathione, fructose, glucose and 5 mg/mL pepsin, papain, bromelain (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 100 μM Cr(VI) (red bars).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the DECDs (black line) and that of Cr(VI) (red line). Inset was the UV–vis spectra of the DECDs upon addition of various concentration of Cr(VI) ( from bottom to up: 0, 15, 25, 40, 60, 100 μM). (B) Suppressed efficiency (E, %) of observed (E obsd) and corrected (E cor) fluorescence intensity at 430 nm, with removal of IFE from E obsd measurements for the DECDs after each addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI).
(A) Preparation procedure of the DECDs; (B) Schematic diagram for Cr(VI) sensing.
Determination results of Cr(VI) in textile samples.
Determination results of Cr(VI) in steel and chromium residue samples.
Determination results of Cr(VI) in industrial wastewater.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of chromium(VI) in real samples based on dual emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As we know, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) was usually used as an additive to improve the color fastness during the printing and dyeing process, and thus posing tremendous threat to our health and living quality. In this work, the dual emissive carbon dots (DECDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of m-aminophenol and oxalic acid. The obtained DECDs not only exhibited dual emission fluorescence peaks (430 nm, 510 nm) under the single excitation wavelength of 380 nm, but also possessed good water solubility and excellent fluorescence stability. A ratiometric fluorescent method for the determination of Cr(VI) was developed using the DECDs as a probe. Under the optimal conditions, a linear range was obtained from 2 to 300 μM with a limit of detection of 0.4 μM. Furthermore, the proposed ratiometric fluorescent method was applied to the analysis of Cr(VI) in textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium residue samples with satisfactory recoveries (88.4–106.8%).

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, studies on the highly selective detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) have been paid much attention due to its wide applications in a variety of industrial processes, such as electroplating, leather tanning, dyestuff and wood preserving [1]. As an additive, a large number of Cr(VI) are often added into textile during printing and dyeing process in order to improve color fastness. Thus, Cr(VI) was very closely related to our daily life. As we know, Cr(VI) was hazardous to public health because of its mutagenicity and carcinogenicity [2]. Notably, Cr(VI) can easily permeate the cell membrane by nonspecific phosphate and sulfate anions transport mechanism, the as generated reactive intermediates such as Cr(V), Cr(IV) and oxygen radicals could result DNA mutation [3,4]. According to the OEKO-TEX standard 100, the permitted concentration of Cr(VI) in textile is strictly regulated to 1 mg/kg [5]. Hence, ultrasensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) in textile is of great importance not only for examining and controlling the quality of textile, but also for taking the edge off the risks of exposed environment and consumers.

To date, numerous analytical methods including atomic absorption spectrometry [6], gas chromatography [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8] and electrochemistry [9] were utilized to quantify the level of Cr(VI). Nevertheless, problems such as tedious sample pretreatments and complicated instruments have largely limited the development of Cr(VI) determination. Recently, fluorometric approaches have been employed to analyze Cr(VI) owning to their high sensitivity, simplicity, and low cost. For example, Zhang et al. prepared water-soluble CdTe quantum dots for the determination of Cr(VI) [10]. Zhang et al. developed 1,8-naphthalimide-based fluorescent probe for Cr(VI) sensing with sensitivity [11]. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to excellent properties, such as facile synthesis route, bright fluorescence and excellent chemical stability [12,13]. These unique properties make CDs be widely favored by researchers in photocatalysis [14,15], sensors [16–18], bioimaging [19–21] and drug delivery [22]. Over the past few years, a series of fluorescent methods for the detection of Cr(VI) have been developed by using CDs [23–25]. For example, Zheng et al. synthesized CDs by thermal pyrolysis route for sensing of Cr(VI) [23]. Fang et al. developed one-step hydrothermal approach to prepare CDs for the detection of Cr(VI) [24]. However, Cr(VI) detection through using these reported CDs was almost based on the change of single-emission fluorescence signal. These single-emission probes used for detection could be influenced by a lot of factors, including the probe concentration, the inhomogeneous distribution, the variation of light-source intensity, and other systematical and environmental aspects, which may result in inaccurate or false results [26,27]. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescent methods could avoid all of these above disadvantages by calculating the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two different emission wavelengths [28]. However, the traditional construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes required combining different fluorescent emission of nanomaterials through chemical or physical strategies, and the fabrication process was much more tedious. The emergence of dual emissive carbon dots (DECDs) would simplify the construction process. To the best of our knowledge, determination of Cr(VI) by ratiometric fluorescent probe has not been reported. Consequently, it is still in high demand to design a simple ratiometric fluorescent probe for accurate determination of Cr(VI).

In this work, the CDs exhibiting dual emission peaks centered at 430 nm and 510 nm were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of m-aminophenol and oxalic acid ( Scheme 1A). As expected, the as prepared DECDs could serve as an inner filter effect (IFE)-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cr(VI) sensing with high selectivity (Scheme 1B). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) based on the DECDs. Significantly, the DECDs probe was successfully applied for the determination of Cr(VI) in different textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium residue samples.

Unless other notation, all reagents were of analytical grade and used without purification. L-histidine hydrochloride monohydrate and m-aminophenol were bought from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). K2Cr2O7, KCl, NaF, NaBr, NaI, NaNO3, Na2SO4, KSCN, (NH4)2C2O4, CuCl2, FeCl3 and CrCl3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl and MnCl2 were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd (Chengdu, China). Oxalic acid, NaH2PO4, Na2CO3, Na2HPO4, CaCl2, MgCl2, CoCl2, BaCl2, AlCl3, catechol and resorcinol were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). NiCl2 was bought from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). CdCl2 was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycine, alanine, cysteine, glutathione, fructose, glucose, pepsin, papain and bromelain were from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout all the experiments from 18202 V AXL water purification system (Chongqing, China).

The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were surveyed on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer with a 1 cm quartz cell (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared spectrum was carried out by a Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer (FT-IR, America) with KBr pellets. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the slit width for excitation and emission were set at 5 nm with a 1 cm quartz cell (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The morphology and size of the prepared DECDs were performed by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a Hitachi-600 transmission electron micro-scope (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurements were made on a PHI 5702 spectrometer. The quantum yield and the fluorescent lifetime were conducted in a time-correlated-single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system from FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., U.K.).

The synthetic procedure of DECDs was described as follows: firstly, 54.5 mg m-aminophenol and 32.0 mg oxalic acid were added into 5 mL ultrapure water. The above mixture was heated by water bath to form a transparent solution under vigorous stirring. Then, the obtained solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After heating at 180 °C in an oven for 12 h and cooling down to room temperature, a light yellow solution was obtained. Subsequently, the product was filtered through 0.22 µm membrane to remove the large particles. Finally, the collected DECDs solution was kept at room temperature for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn off” assay for the detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cr(VI) was performed in PBS buffer solution. In a typical detection, 100 μL of the DECDs solution was placed in 2.85 mL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0). Then, 30 μL of Cr(VI) solution with different concentrations was added to above solution. After incubation for 30 s at room temperature, the corresponding fluorescence emission spectrum was measured with the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The working curve was mapped by the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 510 /F 430) of the DECDs vs the concentration of Cr(VI), F 510 was the fluorescence intensity centered at 510 nm, F 430 was the fluorescence intensity centered at 430 nm.

For the selectivity test toward Cr(VI), different metal ions (i.e., K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+), anion ions (i.e.,
F
-
,
Cl
-
,
Br
-
,
I
-
,
NO
3
-
,
H
2
PO
4
-
,
SCN
-
,
SO
4
2 -
,
CO
3
2 -
,
HPO
4
2 -
and
C
2
O
4
2 -
) and organic as well as biomolecules (catechol, resorcinol, glycine, alanine, cysteine, glutathione, fructose, glucose, pepsin, papain and bromelain) were chosen to instead of Cr(VI) under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cr(VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Textile samples were prepared according to the previous report [5]. Cotton, polyester, and acrylic were purchased randomly from a local market (Lanzhou, China). Briefly, the samples were cut into pieces smaller than 5 × 5 mm. An accurately weighed minced sample was transferred to a triangular flask and 30 mL the acid artificial perspiration solution (0.5 g/L L-histidine hydrochloride monohydrate, 5.0 g/L sodium chloride and 2.2 g/L sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate dehydrate) was added. After shaking for 60 min by a constant-temperature shaker at 37 °C, the mixture was filtered with a 0.45 µm pore size filter membrane. The steel sample was prepared according to the previous report [29]. The steel sample (0.25 g) was mixed with 2.5 mL of concentrated HNO3 and 7.5 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was heated to dissolve, if the mixture was insoluble, the HCl was added into the above solution to help dissolve. Subsequently, the solution was cooled to room temperature and then diluted 10 times using PBS buffer. The industrial wastewater was collected from the effluent of an electroplating factory. The sample was filtered through a 0.45 µm pore size filter membrane. The chromium reside sample was prepared according to the previous report [30]. An accurately weighed chromium residue was transferred to a beaker and 50 mL the digestion reagent (20 g/L sodium hydroxide and 30 g/L sodium carbonate), 0.4 g magnesium chloride, and 0.5 mL phosphate buffer (0.5 mol/L monopotassium phosphate and 0.5 mol/L potassium monodrate, pH = 7.0) were added. Then, stirring the sample with glass bar for at least 5 min at room temperature, the mixture was heated for 1 h at 95 °C. The heating process required to stir to avoid spilling, boiling or evaporating the sample. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the volume was adjusted to 50 mL with ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 4000 r/min for 5 min, and the supernatant was removed. The remaining sample was continuously stirred and concentrated HNO3 was slowly added. The pH of the digested sample was measured with a pH meter. If the pH was less than 7.0, the digestion process was resumed. If the pH was higher than 8.0, dilute HNO3 was added and the digestion process was resumed. Finally, the sample was diluted to 200 mL with ultrapure water.

For the fluorescence analysis of Cr(VI) in real samples, 30 μL of a Cr(VI)-spiked sample solution was added into the DECDs solution, and they were mixed thoroughly. After 30 s, fluorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The content of Cr(VI) was calculated according to a linear equation obtained from a standard solution of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual emissive carbon dots (DECDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, m-aminophenol and oxalic acid were utilized to synthesize the DECDs through hydrothermal method. The reason for selecting m-aminophenol as the starting material in the reaction system as follows: Firstly, the strong donor groups (−OH and –NH2) of m-aminophenol usually causes dramatic changes in fluorescent parameters (mainly including the intersystem crossing rate constant) and further increases the fluorescence quantum yield [31]. Secondly, on comparing the fluorescence spectra of DECDs and o-CDs or p-CDs by using o-aminophenol or p-aminophenol instead of m-aminophenol in the same reaction system, the DECDs exhibited unique dual emissive wavelengths at 430 nm and 510 nm, whereas the o-CDs or p-CDs had only one broad emission peak (Fig. S1).

During the synthesis of CDs, the polymer clusters are first formed by dehydration of the small molecules. As the reaction temperature increases, the CDs are then formed [32]. Thus, the possible process of forming the DECDs was shown in Fig. S2. The m-aminophenol and oxalic acid underwent a continuous dehydration process to form polymer clusters, which were further carbonized to form the DECDs under the high reaction temperature.

From Fig. S1, the three kinds of CDs showed different fluorescent properties, indicating the molecular structure of precursor played an important role in controlling the fluorescent property of DECDs. In addition, the formation of more carboxyl groups may lead to a red-shift of the fluorescence emission peak [33]. The m-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of m-aminophenol, the m-CDs had only one emission peak at about 430 nm (Fig. S3). For the DECDs, the emission peak at 510 nm appeared in the presence of oxalic acid as the starting material. As a result, carboxyl group related fluorescence center might lead to an emission peak at 510 nm in DECDs.

Many reports regarded the dual emissive bands of CDs were attributed to carbon core and surface state [34,35]. Some studies had suggested that the carbon core possessed lower QY and good photostability, while the surface state owned strong fluorescence emission with high QY [32,36]. In this paper, the strongest blue emission was observed for DECDs with a QY of 2.5% whereas DECDs showed strongest green emission with a QY of 42.2%. These results indicated that the appearance of dual emissive wavelengths might be assigned to the carbon core and surface state.

Then, TEM was carried out to characterize the morphology and size of the DECDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1A, DECDs were spherical in shape and well separated from each other. The size of DECDs was in the range of 2.2 − 4.1 nm, with an average size of approximately 3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). The corresponding histogram obtained by statistical analysis of approximately 50 particles in Fig. 1A. Subsequently, DECDs were characterized by XPS and FT-IR to identify their surface composition and chemical state. The XPS in the full range proved the existence of C 1s (283.6 eV), N 1s (399.1 eV) and O 1s (532.1 eV) (Fig. S4). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum ( Fig. 2A) revealed the signals of C-C/C˭C at 284.6 eV and C-N/C-O at 285.7 eV [37,38]. The N 1s band (Fig. 2B) could be deconvolved into two peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.5 eV, representing the existence of -NH2 and C-N, respectively [39]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) could be resolved as follows: -OH at 532.2 eV, C-O at 533.2 eV [40]. In FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2D), the distinct absorption peaks at 3360 cm−1 and 3036 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of -OH and -NH2 [41], and the peak at 1600 cm−1 came from the vibration of C˭O [42], and the absorption peak at 1467 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of C˭C [43]. In addition, the existence of two peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 1119 cm−1 represented the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of C-O, respectively [44]. The appearance of 1263 cm−1 assigned to the bending vibration of C-NH [45,46]. Taken together, results of FT-IR and XPS illustrated the successful incorporation of nitrogen into the DECDs.

The UV–vis spectrum of the DECDs was shown in Fig. 3A. Obviously, the DECDs exhibited an evident absorption band located at 280 nm, corresponding to π-π* transition of C˭C groups, which did not result in long-wavelength fluorescence [47]. It should be noted that the absorption further extended to the visible region, and particularly the existence of two obvious absorption bands around 442 nm and 490 nm, such unique absorption feature was probably owning to different kinds of electron transitions and the introduction of the corresponding functional groups [48]. Unlike most previously reported CDs, the obtained DECDs possessed an excitation-independent feature. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence spectra exhibited two peaks at 430 nm and 510 nm when the excitation wavelength was between 350 nm and 390 nm. Interestingly, the dual emission peaks were not shifted under different excitation wavelengths and the higher fluorescence intensity at 510 nm, indicating that emission properties of DECDs mostly depended on the surface state [19]. With further increasing of the excitation wavelength (400–470 nm), only single emission peak (centered at about 510 nm) was observed. These results indicated that the two well resolved blue and green fluorescence peaks (430 nm, 510 nm) with the excitation wavelength of 380 nm might be originated from different electronic transition process induced by carbon core and surface states of the functional groups [36,49]. From Fig. 3B, emission intensity of the DECDs was all influenced by excitation wavelength. When 350 nm or 360 nm was chosen as excitation wavelength, the emission peak at 510 nm was not apparent, which was not conducive to the ratiometric fluorescent detection. In addition, the fluorescence was quenched by introducing Cr(VI) into the detection system and the quenching efficiencies were not much difference. Thus, the difference of fluorescence intensities at 430 nm and 510 nm were also not much difference. When 370 nm or 390 nm was chosen as excitation wavelength, the difference of fluorescence intensity at 430 nm and 510 nm was relatively large. In order to ensure the sensitivity of the ratiometric fluorescent method, the 380 nm was chosen as the optimum excitation wavelength. Furthermore, absolute fluorescence quantum yield of DECDs was about 5.4% under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The blue and green emission lifetimes of DECDs were 5.5 ns and 4.8 ns, respectively (Fig. S5). Additionally, stability of the DECDs was also studied. From Fig. 4A, Fig. 4B and Fig. 4C, we could see that even the NaCl concentration was as high as 100 mM, the light illumination time was 1 h under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, or the temperature increased from 25 to 85 °C, the F 510/F 430 was almost unchanged, indicating the excellent stability of the DECDs under different environmental conditions. Fig. 4D presented the influence of different pH values on F 510/F 430, the F 510/F 430 of the DECDs gradually decreased with pH values from 4.0 to 7.0, but remained almost constant when the pH value was higher than 7.0. These results may be caused by the terminal amino groups on the surface of the DECDs [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response property of DECDs toward Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Sensitivity of the detection of Cr(VI) was strongly related to the detection conditions including pH of the buffer solution and incubation time. It should be noted that with the pH values ranging from 3.0 to 8.0, the difference value of fluorescence intensity ratio ((F 510/F 430)−(F 510/F 430)0) reached a maximum when pH was 7.0. The change of F 510/F 430 was quite rapid and achieved equilibrium within 30 s. On the basis of those results (Fig. S6), the analyte system was incubated for 30 s under a pH of 7.0.

Subsequently, under the optimal conditions, the capability of the DECDs to detect Cr(VI) was examined. As seen in Fig. 5A, with the increasing concentration of Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity of DECDs centered at 430 nm and 510 nm gradually decreased. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent probe with a sensitive response to Cr(VI) was established. As displayed in Fig. 5B, F 510/F 430 exhibited a good linear relationship to the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range of 2 − 300 μM (
R
2
= 0.999
). Moreover, the limit of detection was calculated to be 0.4 μM based on a 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot) [51]. It is noteworthy that compared with other colorimetric and fluorescent probes, although their absolute fluorescence quantum yield was relatively low, the DECDs possessed fast response time and a more wide linear range (Table S1).

The complexity of the real samples was considered to be the greatest challenge in selectivity investigation. Thus, different metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Cr(VI)), anion ions (
F
-
,
Cl
-
,
Br
-
,
I
-
,
NO
3
-
,
H
2
PO
4
-
,
SCN
-
,
SO
4
2 -
,
CO
3
2 -
,
HPO
4
2 -
and
C
2
O
4
2 -
) and organic as well as biomolecules (catechol, resorcinol, glycine, alanine, cysteine, glutathione, fructose, glucose, pepsin, papain and bromelain) were chosen as the potential interferences. As shown in Fig. 6, only the addition of Cr(VI) caused an obvious fluorescence quenching, and others almost had no influence on the F 510/F 430. Subsequently, a certain concentration of Cr(VI) and the potential interferences were added together into the detection system, the fluorescent response of the DECDs probe to Cr(VI) displayed minimum interference. These results suggested the excellent selectivity of the proposed method, and provided a good precondition for Cr(VI) detection in real samples.

As presented in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of the emission wavelength (λ em = 430 nm) gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of Cr(VI). Additionally, Cr(VI) exhibited broad absorption peaks centered at 260 nm, 360 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The wide absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) almost fully covered with that of DECDs and the absorbance was increased with the increase of Cr(VI) concentrations. It suggests that the Cr(VI) induced fluorescence quenching of the DECDs may stem from the IFE. In order to further verify this hypothesis, corrections are made by taking into consideration the cuvette geometry and absorption characteristics of the mixtures of DECDs and Cr(VI) with Eq. (1) [52]:
(1)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 - 10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 - 10
- s
A
em
Where F obsd is the observed maximum fluorescence intensity and F cor is the corrected maximum fluorescence intensity by deducting IFE from F obsd; A ex and A em represent the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λ ex = 380 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λ em = 430 nm), respectively. As presented in Fig. S7, s is the thickness of excitation beam (0.10 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.40 cm in this case), and d is the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm). The maximum value of the correction factor could not exceed 3; otherwise, the correction is not convincing [43]. Table S2 summarized the concentration of Cr(VI), the correction factor (CF) of IFE, the observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiencies (E obsd and E cor) after addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI) into DECDs solution, respectively. The results demonstrated that approximately 95% of the suppressed efficiency came from the IFE ( Fig. 7B). Similarly, the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm exhibited a similar mechanism in which the fluorescence quenching efficiency stemmed from IFE. Moreover, the good selectivity might be attributed to IFE. To prove this assumption, the UV–vis spectra of different metal ions, anion ions as well as organic and biomolecules were studied. As shown in Fig. S8, only the absorption bands of Cr (VI) had an obvious overlap with the emission band and the exciting light of the DECDs. Thus, this ratiometric fluorescent method exhibited the excellent selectivity.

In addition, the fluorescence quenching of CDs against Cr(VI) might involve the formation of specific bond and electron transfer process [53]. In order to confirm that this fluorescence quenching from the formation of specific bond, FT-IR spectra of DECDs with and without Cr(VI) were employed to investigate this interaction. It could be seen from FT-IR spectra（Fig. S9）that the main absorption peaks have no obvious change in the absence and present of Cr(VI). This result indicated that no specific bond was formed. In addition, XPS spectra of DECDs with and without Cr(VI) were used to verify the electron transfer process. As shown in the Table S3, The binding energy of different elements of the DECDs were nearly invariant before and after adding Cr(VI). This result indicated there was no electron transfer process. It is noteworthy that the color of DECDs solution had a little change without and with Cr(VI) (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Taking advantage of the good properties of excellent selectivity of the proposed detection system, the practicability of the probe was further confirmed. Herein, applications of the DECDs probe for Cr(VI) detection in textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium reside samples were investigated. As shown in
Tables 1, 2 and 3, the amounts of Cr(VI) in some real samples, such as steel, industrial wastewater, and chromium residue, were 0.09 mg/g, 0.22 mg/L, and 12.66 mg/g, respectively. When the textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium residue samples were spiked with different Cr(VI) concentrations and measured with the current method, quantitative spike recoveries for the detection of Cr(VI) in these above real samples ranged from 88.4% to 106.8% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 5% for three replicate measurements. These results further confirmed its reliability and feasibility for monitoring Cr(VI) in textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium residue samples.

In summary, a unique approach was presented to fabricate DECDs using m-aminophenol and oxalic acid as the starting materials by hydrothermal method. Importantly, a ratiometric fluorescent probe for Cr(VI) was developed based on the DECDs. Compared with previous works, this method exhibited some appealing properties, such as ratiometric detection, environmental friendliness, wide linear range, and excellent selectivity of Cr(VI) determination. Notably, the fabricated ratiometric fluorescent probe has been successfully used for the analysis of Cr(VI) in textile, steel, industrial wastewater and chromium residue samples. It was proved to be satisfactory, and the data obtained fully indicated that this proposed method was useful in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675068).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.03.081.
